description: Steve and I had been observing multiple terns feeding over the reservoir as well as a few perched fairly close to us. I noticed a bird begin to fly in towards us and got great looks at it and a common tern in close proximity. I at first took note of the limited dark primary tips of the bird and after viewing multiple COTE I noticted the bird looked somewhat different. The bird made a quick attempt to land on a small sandspit when it outstreached its legs I thought they were noticably small. Also, the black in the primaries was somewhat reduced and I got Steve onto the bird. Unfortunately the bird then began to flay away from us. I then was able to view the bird in my scope for about a minute before it flew into another very distant cove. The most obvious mark in flight was a white flash on the back of the wing extending from the base of the wing and and trailing off after the joint in the wing. The white flash is reminicent of the wing color shown in Forster's Tern and Bonaparte's Gull. The bird flew nearly straight away from us before turning and dissappearing. While hoping the bird would turn around I took note of its flight stlye. While maintaing a constant height the bird appeared to bounce, meaning the body would bob up and down as the bird would beat its wings. This flight stlye is distinct from the much more constant flight stlye I expect from Common Tern.
